event_text: Bartolomeu Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope, opening a sea route around southern Africa.
Vasco da Gama sails around the Cape on his voyage to India.
The Dutch East India Company establishes a refreshment station at the Cape under Jan van Riebeeck.
The first French Huguenot refugees begin arriving at the Cape, with larger arrivals following in 1688.
Britain occupies the Cape Colony during the French Revolutionary Wars.
Britain takes permanent control of the Cape after the Battle of Blaauwberg.
British settlers arrive in the eastern Cape.
Ordinance 50 grants legal rights to Khoikhoi and other free people of colour in the Cape.
Slavery is abolished in the Cape Colony.
Boer groups begin leaving the Cape Colony in the movement later known as the Great Trek.
Voortrekker forces defeat Zulu forces at the Battle of Blood River.
Britain annexes Natal.
The Sand River Convention recognises the independence of the South African Republic.
The Bloemfontein Convention recognises the Orange Free State.
Diamonds are discovered near the Vaal River.
Britain annexes the South African Republic, increasing tensions with Boers.
The Anglo-Zulu War is fought, including Isandlwana and Rorke’s Drift.
The First Boer War begins after Boer republicans proclaim renewed independence for the Transvaal.
Gold is discovered on the Witwatersrand, transforming the regional economy.
The Jameson Raid increases tensions before the Second Boer War.
The Second Boer War begins between Britain and the Boer republics.
The Treaty of Vereeniging ends the Second Boer War.
The Union of South Africa is established.
The South African Native National Congress, later the ANC, is founded.
The Natives Land Act severely restricts Black land ownership.
South Africa enters the First World War as part of the British Empire.
The Maritz Rebellion challenges South Africa’s entry into the First World War.
The Rand Rebellion erupts among white mine workers.
The Statute of Westminster increases South Africa’s autonomy within the British Commonwealth.
South Africa participates in the Second World War.
The National Party wins government and begins formal apartheid policy.
The Population Registration Act classifies South Africans by race.
The Defiance Campaign challenges apartheid laws.
The Freedom Charter is adopted at Kliptown.
The Treason Trial targets anti-apartheid activists.
Police kill protesters at Sharpeville.
South Africa becomes a republic and leaves the Commonwealth.
Umkhonto we Sizwe is formed as the ANC’s armed wing.
The Rivonia Trial leads to life sentences for Nelson Mandela and others.
Prime Minister Hendrik Verwoerd is assassinated.
The Soweto uprising becomes a turning point in resistance to apartheid.
Steve Biko dies in police custody.
The United Democratic Front is formed to oppose apartheid.
A state of emergency is declared amid escalating unrest.
F. W. de Klerk becomes state president.
Nelson Mandela is released and the ANC is unbanned.
Key apartheid laws are repealed.
An interim constitution is agreed during negotiations to end apartheid.
South Africa holds its first democratic election with universal adult suffrage.
South Africa wins the Rugby World Cup on home soil.
South Africa adopts a new democratic constitution.
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission presents its report.
Thabo Mbeki becomes president.
South Africa celebrates ten years of democracy.
Xenophobic attacks affect communities across South Africa.
South Africa hosts the FIFA World Cup.
Police kill striking miners at Marikana.
Nelson Mandela dies.
Cyril Ramaphosa becomes president after Jacob Zuma resigns.
The COVID-19 pandemic brings major restrictions and economic pressure.
Civil unrest follows the imprisonment of Jacob Zuma.
